hook: You already know how to read a bar graph, a line graph, and a scatter plot — but do you know which one to reach for when you need to display a new set of data? Imagine you have two different data sets: one tracking a student's test scores over six months, and another comparing the favorite sports of 200 students. Using the wrong display could make your data impossible to read or even misleading. So how do you decide which display is the right tool for the job?
concept: Choosing an appropriate data display means matching the type of data and the question you want to answer to the graph that communicates it most clearly. Different displays are designed for different purposes: bar graphs compare categories, line graphs show change over time, histograms show frequency distributions of numerical data, scatter plots reveal relationships between two numerical variables, and circle (pie) graphs show parts of a whole. The goal is always to let the data tell its story as clearly as possible.
anatomy: Every data display decision involves three key components: the data type (categorical, such as colors or sports, versus numerical, such as heights or temperatures), the relationship being investigated (comparison across groups, change over time, distribution of values, or correlation between two variables), and the audience's question (what does the viewer need to understand at a glance?). A bar graph has categorical labels on one axis and a frequency or value scale on the other; a line graph has a time or ordered variable on the horizontal axis and a measured value on the vertical axis; a scatter plot plots ordered pairs $(x, y)$ where both variables are numerical; a histogram uses connected bars over numerical intervals called bins; and a circle graph divides 360° into sectors proportional to each category's percentage of the whole.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Data: Number of books read per month by one student over 8 months}
worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying what kind of data we have. The variable 'number of books' is numerical, and 'month' is an ordered time variable — this immediately tells us the data has a sequence that matters, which is a strong signal we are looking at change over time.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Question: How did reading habits change over the 8 months?}
worked_example.description_latex: Naming the question we want to answer is the most important step. Whenever the question asks about trend, growth, or change across an ordered sequence (especially time), a line graph is the appropriate display because the connected line visually communicates the direction and rate of change.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Eliminate: bar graph, scatter plot, circle graph, histogram}
worked_example.description_latex: We rule out other displays by checking them against our data and question: a bar graph would compare separate months without showing the trend between them; a scatter plot is used when both variables are numerical and unordered; a circle graph is for part-to-whole relationships; and a histogram is for grouped frequency distributions, not sequential monthly counts.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Best display: Line graph with months on the } x\text{-axis and books read on the } y\text{-axis}
worked_example.description_latex: We conclude that a line graph is the best choice because it is specifically designed to display how a numerical value changes over an ordered sequence, and the connected line makes any upward or downward trend immediately visible to the reader.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Data: The percentage of students who prefer each of 5 school lunch options}
partial_example.description_latex: Begin by identifying the data type. We have five categories (lunch options), and for each category we know its share of the whole student population expressed as a percentage. Ask yourself: are we comparing parts of a single whole, or comparing separate groups?

Notice that the five percentages must add up to exactly 100%, which means the data represents parts of a single whole. This is the defining feature that separates circle graph situations from bar graph situations — when every category is a slice of the same pie, the circle graph is designed for exactly this structure.

Now it is your turn: name the most appropriate display for this data and explain in one sentence why it fits better than a bar graph would. Think about what feature of a circle graph directly represents 'part of a whole.'

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A teacher records the high temperature (°F) in her city for each of the 12 months of the year.} \\ \text{Which display is most appropriate: bar graph, line graph, or circle graph?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Line graph — temperature is a numerical value changing over ordered time (months).}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A survey asks 150 students to name their favorite subject. The results are:} \\ \text{Math: 40, Science: 35, English: 30, History: 25, Art: 20.} \\ \text{Which display best compares the number of students who prefer each subject?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Bar graph — we are comparing counts across separate, unordered categories.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A researcher collects data on students' hours of sleep per night and their scores on a math test.} \\ \text{She wants to know whether more sleep is associated with higher scores.} \\ \text{Which display should she use?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Scatter plot — both variables are numerical and she is investigating a relationship (correlation) between them.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A store records how many customers arrive each hour. The hourly counts for one day are:} \\ 5, 12, 20, 35, 42, 38, 30, 18, 10, 6. \\ \text{A manager wants to see the distribution of busy vs. slow hours grouped into intervals.} \\ \text{Which display is most appropriate, and what would the horizontal axis represent?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Histogram — the horizontal axis shows numerical intervals (bins) of customer counts,} \\ \text{and the vertical axis shows frequency. This reveals the distribution shape.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A school budget of }\$500{,}000\text{ is divided as follows:} \\ \text{Salaries: }60\%,\text{ Supplies: }15\%,\text{ Technology: }10\%,\text{ Facilities: }10\%,\text{ Other: }5\%. \\ \text{A principal wants to show parents how the budget is divided among categories.} \\ \text{(a) Which display is most appropriate?} \\ \text{(b) What angle (in degrees) would the 'Salaries' sector have in that display?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{(a) Circle (pie) graph — the data represents parts of a single whole.} \\ \text{(b) } 0.60 \times 360^\circ = 216^\circ

common_mistakes: Students use a bar graph instead of a histogram when data is numerical and continuous (like test score ranges), not categorical. The fix: if the horizontal axis labels are numerical intervals (e.g., 60–69, 70–79) rather than named categories, a histogram with connected bars is correct because the intervals are ordered and touching.

Students choose a line graph whenever they see numbers, even when the horizontal axis has unordered categories (like favorite colors or sports). The fix: a line graph is only appropriate when the horizontal axis represents an ordered sequence — most commonly time — because the slope of the connecting line implies change between consecutive points.

Students confuse a circle graph with a bar graph when comparing category percentages, even when the values do NOT sum to 100%. The fix: a circle graph is only valid when the categories are mutually exclusive parts of a single whole that together account for 100%; if respondents could pick more than one option, a bar graph is correct because the percentages would exceed 100%.
untested_variants: Dot plots and stem-and-leaf plots as displays for small numerical data sets — the practice problems do not ask students to choose between a histogram and a dot plot when data sets are small.
Two-way frequency tables and their relationship to double bar graphs — the practice problems do not cover situations where data is cross-classified by two categorical variables simultaneously.

Box-and-whisker plots (box plots) as a display for showing the five-number summary and spread of a data set — none of the practice problems involve comparing distributions across groups using box plots.

Misleading data displays — situations where a student must identify WHY a given display is inappropriate or distorted (e.g., a broken y-axis or a 3-D pie chart that distorts proportions) are not covered in the practice problems.
